When you reflect a shape, you flip it over a mirror line or line of symmetry without turning it or changing the size.
The mirror image of a shape.

reflect-polygons-in-a-line-of-symmetry.lessonTitle: Reflect polygons in a line of symmetry

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Children may translate or copy a shape rather than 'flipping it' over the line of symmetry.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Use mirrors to support children's understanding of how a shape flips when it is reflected. Explore this idea with a wide variety of shapes.

reflect-polygons-in-a-line-of-symmetry.pupilLessonOutcome: I can reflect polygons in a line of symmetry, including when there are no sides parallel to the line of symmetry.

teacherTips.teacherTip: Although children are taught different strategies to reflect shapes in this lesson, they may benefit from using a mirror to support their understanding of reflective symmetry.

reflect-polygons-in-a-line-of-symmetry.unitTitle: Properties of 2D and 3D shapes and symmetry

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: When you reflect a shape, each vertex will be the same distance from the line of symmetry.
When you reflect a polygon that has a side that is touching the mirror line, you can sometimes create a new shape.

lessonKeywords.description: If you were to fold a shape on its line of symmetry, both halves would match exactly.
Mirror line is another term to describe the line of symmetry.
When you reflect a shape, you flip it over a mirror line or line of symmetry without turning it or changing the size.
The reflection of a shape is the mirror image of a shape.

reflect-polygons-that-are-dissected-by-t.lessonTitle: Reflect polygons that are dissected by the line of symmetry

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Children may miscount the number of squares from the mirror line when plotting each vertex to reflect the shape.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Encourage children to work systematically by only marking one vertex at a time to reduce the chance of errors. Also encourage use of mirrors to check.

reflect-polygons-that-are-dissected-by-t.pupilLessonOutcome: I can reflect polygons that are dissected (cut in half) by the line of symmetry, and explore the new shapes that are created when a polygon is reflected.

teacherTips.teacherTip: Although this lesson has been designed to teach other strategies for reflecting in a line of symmetry, children may benefit from being able to use mirrors to support their understanding of reflective symmetry.

reflect-polygons-that-are-dissected-by-t.unitTitle: Properties of 2D and 3D shapes and symmetry

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: A pair of compasses can be used to draw a circle with a particular radius.
A pair of compasses can be used to draw two circles that intersect.
A pair of compasses can be used to draw two circles that only intersect once.

lessonKeywords.description: The radius is any line segment that joins the centre of a circle to its edge.
A pair of compasses is a tool which can be used to draw circles and arcs. A pair of compasses is sometimes referred to just as a compass.

securing-the-skill-of-using-a-pair-of-co.lessonTitle: Securing the skill of using a pair of compasses

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Pupils may find it difficult to decide where to mark the centre point of each circle during Task A.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Pupils could start the task by drawing a pair of circles and labelling it with the question that presents the matching description.

securing-the-skill-of-using-a-pair-of-co.pupilLessonOutcome: I can use a pair of compasses to draw different circles.

teacherTips.teacherTip: For this lesson, pupils will need a pair of compasses, pencil, ruler and several sheets of plain paper.

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: Area and perimeter are calculated from the side lengths of 2D shapes.
Area is measured in square units.
Perimeter is measured in units of length.

lessonKeywords.description: Area is a measurement of a flat surface. It measures a 2D space.
The distance around a 2D shape is its perimeter.

shapes-with-the-same-areas-can-have-diff.lessonTitle: Shapes with the same areas can have different perimeters and vice versa

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: It is highly common for pupils to mix up area and perimeter or to confuse the notations for the two.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Allow children plenty of opportunity to identify perimeter and area for the same shapes and discuss why the unit of measure is different. You may also wish to build in deliberate errors when modelling or into tasks for children to identify and check.

shapes-with-the-same-areas-can-have-diff.pupilLessonOutcome: I know that shapes with the same area can have different perimeters and vice-versa.

teacherTips.teacherTip: It is assumed that children have had prior experience of area and perimeter and units of measure before this lesson. Consider whether your learners require pre-teaching or a reminder prior to this lesson.

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: A net is a 2D shape that can be folded and joined to create a 3D shape.
There can be more than one way to create a net for a given 3D shape.
Not all arrangements of shapes will form the net of a 3D shape.

lessonKeywords.description: A net is a 2D representation of the faces of a 3D shape, that can be folded up into the 3D object.

the-same-3d-shapes-can-be-composed-from-.lessonTitle: The same 3D shapes can be composed from different 2D nets

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: It is perfectly possible for pupils to be highly numerate but struggle with the spatial awareness needed to visualise / imagine whether a 2D net will fold together to form a 2D shape.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: As part of the lesson design process you may wish to incorporate manipulatives such as construction materials for those pupils whose learning needs scaffolding. As with all scaffolding, aim to move pupils away from this when you can.

the-same-3d-shapes-can-be-composed-from-.pupilLessonOutcome: I can identify different 2D nets which will create the same 3D shape.

teacherTips.teacherTip: If pupils are miscounting the faces of a 2D shape when planning to create its net, provide small sticky notes for them to place on a face when it has been counted.

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: The area of a triangle is equal to half the area of the parallelogram you create from 2 congruent triangles.
Any parallelogram can be decomposed into two congruent triangles.

lessonKeywords.description: If one shape can fit exactly on top of another using rotation, reflection or translation, then the shapes are congruent.
A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with two pairs of parallel and equal sides.

two-congruent-triangles-can-be-arranged-.lessonTitle: Two congruent triangles can be arranged to compose a parallelogram

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: If pupils do not cut a parallelogram from vertex to opposite vertex, they will end up with either two non-congruent shapes or two congruent quadrilaterals.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Modelling is key and this is a useful time to revisit the concept of a shape's diagonal, that is, a line connecting vertex to a non-adjacent vertex.

two-congruent-triangles-can-be-arranged-.pupilLessonOutcome: I can explain how two congruent triangles can arranged to compose a parallelogram.

teacherTips.teacherTip: In this lesson pupils will need to be familiar with triangle types and their properties. Consider how you might scaffold this knowledge for learners if required.

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: A pair of compasses can be used to draw circle with a particular radius.
All the points on the circle will be the same distance from the centre.
The centre of a circle is the point that is the same distance from every point on the circle.
The collection of points an equal distance from a given point form a circle.

lessonKeywords.description: A fixed point is a point that does not change or move.
The circumference of a circle is the perimeter of the circle.
An arc is part of a curve. An arc of a circle is part of the circle’s circumference.
Points A and B are equidistant from a third point C if the distance AC is equal to the distance BC.

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: During tasks, pupils may use a ruler to draw or check whether points are equidistant from a centre.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: While the tasks could be completed with a ruler, it may be quicker, easier and more accurate to use a pair of compasses.

understanding-constructing-a-circle.pupilLessonOutcome: I can recognise a circle as a collection of points equidistant from a fixed point.

teacherTips.teacherTip: The teacher could sit or stand in the middle of a large empty space and ask all pupils to position themselves equidistantly to the teacher. What shape have the pupils formed?

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: A protractor is used to draw and measure angles accurately.
Read the scale from zero on a protractor to draw and measure angles accurately.
Read the scale from zero on a ruler to draw and measure accurately.

lessonKeywords.description: A protractor is an instrument used in measuring or drawing angles.

use-knowledge-of-shape-properties-to-dra.lessonTitle: Use knowledge of shape properties to draw shapes accurately using rulers and protractors

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Pupils can find protractors tricky, sometimes drawing the wrong angle because the outer scale has been used rather than the inner-scale, or vice-versa.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Emphasise estimation through knowledge of angle types. Model discussion of whether an angle is acute or obtuse and use this knowledge to identify which value on which scale is the measurement.

use-knowledge-of-shape-properties-to-dra.pupilLessonOutcome: I can use my knowledge of shape properties to draw shapes accurately using rulers and protractors.

teacherTips.teacherTip: Consider how confident pupils are in using a protractor independently. Independent tasks could be scaffolded further by adapting angles to measure in multiples of 5 or 10

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: Regular shapes have all sides the same length and all angles equal size.
Knowledge of properties of shapes helps to sketch shapes.
Knowledge of properties of shapes helps to identify shapes.

lessonKeywords.description: A regular polygon has all sides equal and all angles equal.
An irregular polygon does not have all sides equal and does not have all angles equal.

use-knowledge-of-shape-properties-to-ske.lessonTitle: Use knowledge of shape properties to sketch and identify shapes

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Pupils might get confused by shapes which are almost polygons - for example, one of the lines is slightly curved - or almost regular - for example, one side is slightly longer than the others.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: These are either not polygons or are irregular polygons. Ensure pupils are clear about that. Examples and non-examples are given, including 'almost' examples. You may wish to include more and display these in your Maths environment.

use-knowledge-of-shape-properties-to-ske.pupilLessonOutcome: I can use my knowledge of shape properties to sketch and identify shapes.

teacherTips.teacherTip: There is a lot of technical language in this lesson. Consider the most effective ways to introduce this to your children and how best to ensure its continued use, such as displaying key vocabulary or developing stem sentences with children.

keyLearningPoints.keyLearningPoint: The area of the compound shape is equal to the total areas of its parts
2D shapes can be decomposed and rearranged in different ways
2D shapes can be decomposed and rearranged in different ways but the total area stays the same

lessonKeywords.description: A compound shape is a shape created using two or more basic shapes.
Area is the measurement of a flat surface. It measures a 2D space.

when-a-2d-shape-is-decomposed-and-the-pa.lessonTitle: When a 2D shape is decomposed and the parts rearranged, the areas remains the same

misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.misconception: Children may assume some shapes have a larger area than others by comparison of a certain dimension. In this lesson, pattern blocks can be arranged to create shapes with very different dimensions.
misconceptionsAndCommonMistakes.response: Encourage pupils to rearrange the composing shapes to prove that the compound shapes have the same area. This can be done effectively with tangrams or pattern blocks, both of which feature heavily in this lesson.

when-a-2d-shape-is-decomposed-and-the-pa.pupilLessonOutcome: I can decompose a 2D shape and create a different shape from the parts and explain why the area of the new compound shape is the same as the original 2D shape.

teacherTips.teacherTip: It is important pupils have opportunities to manipulate shapes in this lesson and tangrams and pattern blocks are used to this effect. If physical versions are unavailable these manipulatives can be found online for a virtual experience.
